coredata.dc:title: High-quality ultra-flat reduced graphene oxide nanosheets with super-robust lubrication performances
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Bao
Zhao, Jun
He, Yongyong
Chen, Guangyan
Li, Yulong
Zhang, Chenhui
Luo, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance graphene has been driven by increasingly growing industrial demands. Due to the compatibility of high-efficient and low-cost, chemical exfoliation of graphite is currently a common synthesis method of graphene. Unfortunately, there is not an effective chemical way to prepare high-quality graphene nanosheets for industry application. Working under a harsh condition is also a challenge for their engineering application. Herein, high-quality ultra-flat reduced graphene oxide (UF#G) nanosheets with near-perfect integrity were synthesized by a chemical exfoliation method. Taking the advantage of metal ion from the oxidant agent, the manganese compounds are in situ uniformly deposited on the surface of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets in a weak alkali environment, thus protecting the nanostructure from being damaged by gaseous products during chemical reduction process. UF#G can reduce the coefficient of friction (COF) and wear depth of the lubricated interfaces by 49% and 93% at a heavy load (2.5 GPa), compared with pure grease (P.G). Especially, the UF#G-based lubricant has superior lubricating stability with long-operating life of more than 216,000 cycles, which is beyond previous results for both state-of-the-art nanocoatings and nanolubricants. UF#G together with the lubricant generate a robust chemical-mixing tribofilm consisting of a carbon-adsorbed layer and a CaSO4-deposited layer, which is contributed to the super-robust lubrication performances. The ultra-flat nanostructure of UF#G further improves the lubrication by interlayer-sliding effect. This work provides high-quality ultra-flat reduced graphene oxide nanosheets enabled by a novel chemical-exfoliation route with super-robust lubrication performances.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis schematic diagram of UF#G and CE#G. With UE#G as the precursor, CE#G is synthesized by means of the thermal stress generated by the rapid evaporation of anhydrous ethanol at high temperature.

Morphologies and physicochemical properties of UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G. (a) SEM and TEM images of UF#G. The insets show the atomic structure of UF#G. (b) SEM and TEM images of CE#G. The insets show the atomic structure of CE#G. (c) SEM and TEM images of IW#G. The insets show the atomic structure of IW#G. (d) Raman spectra of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, and graphite. (e) XPS spectra of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, graphite. (f) XRD spectra of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, graphite. (g) TGA and DTG curves of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, graphite. The test temperature is raised form 30 ℃ to 800 ℃ with a ramp rate of 10 ℃·min−1 in a nitrogen environment.

Tribological properties of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, graphite, and P.G. (a) COF time-varying curves of different lubricants at 25 ℃, 150 N. (b) The influence of temperature on the tribological properties of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, and graphite at 0.5 wt% and 150 N. (c) The influence of contact load on the tribological properties of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, and graphite at 0.5 wt% and 25 ℃. (d) Extreme pressure performance test at ambient temperature (100–800 N). (e) Long-operating of 216,000 cycles (three hours) friction test at high temperature of 100 ℃.

Morphology and EDS spectrum of wear debris of P.G and 0.5 wt% UF#G at 100 ℃, 150 N for 216,000 cycles. (a) Morphology of the wear debris of 0.5 wt% UF#G is granular with the size of approximately 0.2–0.4 μm. (b) Morphology of the wear debris of P.G is block with the size of approximately 20–30 μm. (c) Distribution of iron and oxygen elements in wear debris of UF#G. (d) Distribution of calcium and sulfur elements in wear debris of UF#G. (e) Distribution of carbon, platinum, and silicon elements in wear debris of UF#G. The platinum element is derived from the conductive Pt film sprayed artificially, and the silicon element comes from the sample carrier of silicon wafer.

Tribofilm morphology and component of UF#G and P.G characterized by HRTEM and EDS. (a) Morphology of tribofilm of 0.5 wt% UF#G under 150 N. (b) Detail image of the UF#G tribofilm in the red box area in a. (b1-b4) EDS spectra of Fe, Ca, S, and C corresponding to b. (c) The local enlargement image of the white box area in b. (d) Morphology of tribofilm of P.G under 150 N. (e) Detail image of the P.G tribofilm in the red box area in d. (e1-e4) EDS spectra of Fe, Ca, S, and C corresponding to e. (f) The local enlargement image of the white box area in e. The EDS spectra indicates that the tribofilm consists of CaSO4 layer and amorphous carbon layer.

Morphology evolution schematic diagram of (a) UF#G, (b) CE#G, and (c) IW#G induced by friction. The nanosheets of UF#G overcome the interlayer van der Waal forces and slip between layers. While the nanosheets of CE#G curl inwards to form graphene nano-rolls, and the nanosheets of IW#G aggregate to form graphene nano-clusters, both of which degrade the tribological properties of graphene.
Lubrication performance comparison of different advanced additives and coatings.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a: Mixture of zinc dialkyldithiophosphate and tetraoctylphosphonium bis(2-ethylhexyl) phosphate; b: Polymer brush grafted nanoparticles; c: Crumpled graphene balls; d: Anatase TiO2 modified graphene oxide nanofluid; e: Graphene nanoplatelets and ZrO2 composite additives; f: Oil-miscible protic ionic liquids; g: Carbon dots; h: Polyethyleneimine (PEI) grafted carbon dots; i: Polytetrafluoroethylene@silica core–shell nanoparticles; j: Nanodiamonds; k: TiAlN and WS composite coating; l: PMXene@polytetrafluoroethylene core–shell hybrid; m: Nickel based MoS2 and graphene composite coating; n: Hydrogenated diamond-like carbon coating.

head.ce:title: High-quality ultra-flat reduced graphene oxide nanosheets with super-robust lubrication performances

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance graphene has been driven by increasingly growing industrial demands. Due to the compatibility of high-efficient and low-cost, chemical exfoliation of graphite is currently a common synthesis method of graphene. Unfortunately, there is not an effective chemical way to prepare high-quality graphene nanosheets for industry application. Working under a harsh condition is also a challenge for their engineering application. Herein, high-quality ultra-flat reduced graphene oxide (UF#G) nanosheets with near-perfect integrity were synthesized by a chemical exfoliation method. Taking the advantage of metal ion from the oxidant agent, the manganese compounds are in situ uniformly deposited on the surface of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets in a weak alkali environment, thus protecting the nanostructure from being damaged by gaseous products during chemical reduction process. UF#G can reduce the coefficient of friction (COF) and wear depth of the lubricated interfaces by 49% and 93% at a heavy load (2.5 GPa), compared with pure grease (P.G). Especially, the UF#G-based lubricant has superior lubricating stability with long-operating life of more than 216,000 cycles, which is beyond previous results for both state-of-the-art nanocoatings and nanolubricants. UF#G together with the lubricant generate a robust chemical-mixing tribofilm consisting of a carbon-adsorbed layer and a CaSO4-deposited layer, which is contributed to the super-robust lubrication performances. The ultra-flat nanostructure of UF#G further improves the lubrication by interlayer-sliding effect. This work provides high-quality ultra-flat reduced graphene oxide nanosheets enabled by a novel chemical-exfoliation route with super-robust lubrication performances.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction exists almost in every mechanical system with moving parts, and accounts for more than 40% of the global primary energy consumption ever year [1–2]. Lubrication is one of the most important strategies for enhancing the durability and efficiency of industrial equipment [3–5]. Due to the advantageous self-lubricating and mechanical performances, graphene and its derivatives have been considered as the most promising alternative to the outdated lubricating additive of zinc dialkyl dithiophosphates (ZDDPs) containing phosphorus and sulfur [6–8]. In addition, the history of graphite family materials being used as lubricating medium can be traced back to 1950 s [9,10]. The structural characteristic of graphene plays an important role in the lubricating performances. Pristine graphene nanosheets with structural integrity have excellent lubricating performances even superlubricity via interlayer slippage [11,12]. For comparison, the absence, replacement, and displacement of sp 2 orbital carbon atoms directly impede the interlayer slippage between graphene layers, thus causing lubrication performance deterioration [13–16]. In addition, working under a harsh condition (high temperature, heavy load, and long cycle) is a critical challenge for the application of graphene and its derivatives.

Therefore, preparation of high-quality graphene is the main concern for practical applications. Nevertheless, the realization of the commercial application of graphene currently is suffering from the lack of scalable methods for its high-efficiency, low-cost, and high-quality production [4,11,17]. Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [3,15,17–19], mechanical exfoliation [12,13,20,21], liquid-phase exfoliation [22–29], and chemical exfoliation [4,7,16,30–34] are the most mainstream synthesis methods for graphene and its derivatives. Graphene prepared by CVD and mechanical exfoliation methods has excellent crystal structure and regular physical morphology, which however, are ultra-low production efficiency and expensive cost [11,18,21]. The low yield and inferior quality of liquid-phase exfoliation method also limit the redevelopment of graphene, which is because of not environmentally friendly agents and high-frequency ultrasonic vibration, resulting in small broken fragments and difficult salvage and collection of graphene [26,27]. Due to the compatibility of high-efficient and low-cost, chemical exfoliation of graphite is the commonly used method for the industrial production of graphene [31, 32，34]. In fact, the chemical delimitation of graphite oxide can be traced back to 1950 s-1960 s [35,36]. The modified Hummers method is the most reliable way to prepare graphene in large scale of industrial areas using graphite oxide as precursors [33, 34，36]. Unfortunately, there is not an effective chemical way to prepare high-quality graphene nanosheets like or beyond the mechanical exfoliation. It is because a large amount of gaseous product is released in the process of high-temperature reduction of graphene oxide, thus leading to the structural damage and inferior quality of graphene nanosheets [4,31,34]. Therefore, to achieve high-quality graphene from propagable chemical exfoliation for industrial application is still urgently desired.

In this study, we successfully synthesized high-quality ultra-flat reduced graphene oxide (UF#G) nanosheets through a novel chemical exfoliation method with super-robust lubrication performance. By accurately adjusting the pH value in the process of chemical exfoliation, manganese compounds can be uniformly deposited on the surface of graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets, thus, the virgin ultra-flat nanostructure of graphite with near-perfect integrity can be fully saved in-spite of subsequent vigorous reduction process. The lubrication performance and friction-induced structure evolution of UF#G are explored. This work provides a feasible method to fabricate high-quality rGO nanosheets, which has the great potential for the practical engineering applications, because of their super-robust lubrication performances under harsh conditions (high temperature, heavy load, and long cycle), such as the transmission system lubrication of mining machinery, military equipment, aerospace equipment, and infrastructure machinery [10,16,19].

Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%), sulfuric acid (50 wt%), and petroleum ether were bought from Shanghai Titan Chemical Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 35 wt%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), and potassium hydroxide (KOH) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Commercial graphite and internal wrinkle reduced graphene oxide (IW#G) nanosheets was purchased from The Sixth Element (Changzhou) Materials Technology Co., Ltd. All materials were used as received without further purification.

Due to the excellent colloidal stability (semi-solid state) of grease at ambient temperature [6,7,30], the adverse effect of graphene nanosheets agglomeration on tribological properties can be effectively avoided. Therefore, grease is selected as the lubricating medium for tribological test. The dispersion process of rGO nanosheets in grease and the detection method of dispersion uniformity are detailed in previous work [7,30]. The pure grease (P.G) and rGO nanosheets were dissolved in petroleum ether, respectively. Then, P.G and rGO in the liquid state described above were mixed. Subsequently, the mixture was stirred electromagnetically for 180 min in a water bath at 40 ℃ to uniformly disperse rGO nanosheets in the grease. At this step, the petroleum ether in the mixture was volatilized completely, and the grease returns to a semi-solid state. Further, the grease was homogenized by ultrasonic vibration for 30 min to improve the micro-dispersion of rGO nanosheets. After that, the grease was refined by three-roller milling machine for three times.

The morphologies of materials were visualized using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, SU8240, Japan) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2010, Japan) with accelerating voltages of 15 kV and 200 kV, respectively. Raman spectra analysis was performed using a LabRam HR800 confocal Raman system with 532 nm diode laser excitation at −70 ℃ (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, U.S.A) was employed to analyze the material structure and elements valence of graphene and wear scars equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source operated at 400 W (accelerating voltage 15 kV, filament current 27 mA). X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker, U.S.A) of graphene was conducted with a Co Kα radiation (wavelength λ = 1.790 Å; accelerating voltage 15 kV, filament current 27 mA, 400 W) over the scanning range (2θ) of 10-90° at the scan rate of 1° min−1. Thermogravimetric behaviors of three graphene were characterized using a differential scanning calorimeter ranging from 30 ℃ to 800 ℃ with a ramp rate of 10 ℃•min−1 in a nitrogen environment (DZ-DSC100A, DAZHAN EQUIPMENT, China). And the planet ball milling machine is purchased from Changsha MITR instrument equipment Co., LTD (YXQM-2L, China).

The tribological properties of UF#G, curly edge reduced graphene oxide (CE#G), and IW#G was investigated on a SRV4 friction and wear tester (Optimal, Germany) with ball-disc contact mode. The material of ball and disc is GCr15 bearing steel. All the experiments were carried out in atmospheric environment. The general parameters of tribological experiments are shown in Table S3. The 3D white-light interferometer (Nexview, Zygo, U.S.A) was employed to characterize the morphologies of wear scars. The micro-area analysis of the wear scars and the morphology of the rGO after friction were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8220 Hitachi, Japan) with accelerating voltage of 15 kV. The element content and distribution were examined by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, X-MaxN Oxford, England). To explore the morphology of tribofilm, a focused ion beam-scanning electron microscope (FIB-SEM, LYRA3 Tescan, Netherlands) was employed to prepare the cross-section samples for HRTEM.

UF#G was synthesized with a novel controllable chemical exfoliation method (Fig. 1 ). Under the ice bath condition, 40 g KMnO4 and 10 g graphite powders were successively added to 300 ml concentrated H2SO4 solution. After 20 min of electromagnetic stirring, the temperature of the water bath was slowly raised to 45 ℃ and electromagnetic stirring for another 60 min. In this step, the graphite is slowly oxidized under mild conditions, and the oxygen-containing functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –CHO) are intercalated between the graphite sheets, increasing the interlayers distance of nanosheets. Next, 1000 ml deionized water was slowly added to the mixture, and electromagnetic stirring for 30 min. In this step, graphene oxide (GO) is synthesized and peeled off from the graphite. And then, H2O2 aqueous solution was slowly added into the mixture until the solution changed completely from brown to golden. Excess permanganate ions are reduced by H2O2 at this step. In acidic environment, potassium permanganate reacts with hydrogen peroxide producing manganese sulfate and oxygen [37,38] as explained in Eqs. (1). Subsequently, KOH aqueous solution (50 wt%) was added to the mixture drop by drop, until the pH value of the mixture rose to approximately 8.0. At the beginning of adding KOH solution, the excess sulfuric acid is neutralized as explained in Eqs. (2). Further, excess hydroxide ions regulated the solution to be weakly alkaline (pH≈8.0), and a thin layer of Mn(OH)2 crystal uniformly deposited on the GO nanosheets surface [39,40] as explained in Eqs. (3), maintaining the structural integrity of rGO nanosheets during the subsequent high temperature reduction process. Then the mixture was filtered under low vacuum conditions to collect the solid suspended substance.

The pyrolysis reactions of most oxygen-containing functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –CHO) occur between 150 ℃ and 220 ℃ [4,31,33,41–43]. The product of filtration was then reduced at 180 ℃ for 24 h in a vacuum oven, sufficiently removing the oxygen-containing functional groups of GO [4,16,41,42]. In the reduced process, oxygen-containing functional groups decompose to produce a large amount of high-temperature gas (H2O, CO2, CO), which affects the integrity of rGO nanosheets [7,16,32,41,43]. Because the surface of GO is covered with a layer of Mn(OH)2 crystal, the structure of rGO nanosheets is protected from the damage of gaseous products in reduction process. Mn(OH)2 decomposes into MnO adhering to the surface of rGO nanosheets at high temperature reduction process as explained in Eqs. (4) [44,45]. Subsequently, the dried product was dissolved with H2SO4 solution (50 wt%) and deionized water, respectively. In this step, the Mn(OH)2 and MnO crystals covered on the rGO surface are dissolved by H2SO4 forming soluble MnSO4 as explained in Eqs. (5) and Eqs. (6) [46,47].

After that, the suspension was filtered again, and the filtered product was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 ℃ for 2 h. This purification step was repeated for five iterations (Fig. 1). Whereafter, the final dried product was ball-milled, and the equipment for ball milling and working parameters are shown in Fig. S1 and Table S1, respectively. The planet container revolves at 200 r/min and rotates at 400 r/min for 30 min with the grinding steel balls diameter of 6 mm. After that, approximately 5.0 g UF#G can be obtained. The comparison results of UF#G and graphene prepared by other methods are shown in Table S2. Using UF#G as the precursor, CE#G was synthesized by thermal stress generated by rapid evaporation of anhydrous ethanol at high temperature. Then UF#G was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol and placed in a vacuum oven at 200 ℃ for 30 min. Finally, approximately 4.7 g CE#G powder could be obtained.

The morphology and element distribution of unpickled rGO nanosheets after high temperature reduction is showed in Fig. S2. The Mn(OH)2 nanoparticles deposited on rGO surface in weak alkaline conditions decomposed into MnO nanoparticles during high temperature reduction process (Fig. S2a-d). The (200) and (111) planes of MnO with d-space of 0.221 nm and 0.254 nm can be observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. S2d). The uniform distribution of nanoparticles effectively protects the rGO nanostructure from the destruction of gaseous products (H2O, CO2, CO) during high temperature reduction process (Fig. S2e and S2f).

The overall morphology of the three rGO nanosheets (UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G) and original graphite is shown in Fig. S3. The sizes of all materials are approximately 5–8 μm. UF#G has a parallelogram shape with an extremely flat in-plane structure and regular edge morphology, just like a blade (Fig. 2 a). The inset in Fig. 2a indicates the extremely regular lamellar structure of UF#G. Due to high temperature evaporation of anhydrous ethanol, thermal stress concentration effect occurs at the edge of CE#G nanosheets (Fig. 2b), leading to significant inward coiling at the nanosheets edge. The inset in Fig. 2b indicates the crimped edges and flat interior structure of CE#G. While the nanosheets of IW#G have regular edges, but with a large number of wrinkles and protrusions that resemble mountain ranges at the central region of nanosheets (Fig. 2c). The inset in Fig. 2c shows the irregular wrinkles and protrusion of IW#G.

Raman spectra, XPS spectra, XRD spectra, and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) were performed to systematically compare and analyze the physicochemical properties of three rGO nanosheets and graphite. For Raman studies (Fig. 2d), the D peak (1350 cm−1) is ascribed to breathing modes of six atom rings and associated with the one defect and one in-plane transverse optical phonon [12,48,49]. The G peak (1578 cm−1) is originated from the first-order scattering process, which is closely related to doubly degenerate in-plane transverse optical (iTO) and longitudinal optical (LO) phonon modes at the Brillouin zone center [48]. The position, intensity, and width of the D and G peaks of UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G have been listed in Table S4. The ratio of D peak intensity to G peak intensity ID/IG of UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G are 0.884, 0.975, and 1.135 respectively, indicating that the presence of curly edges and internal wrinkles increases the defect level of rGO nanosheets [48,49].

XPS spectra shows that the major elemental components of rGO and graphite are C 1 s and O 1 s (Fig. 2e). As a results of redox, UF#G and CE#G contain more oxygen element. The residual trace manganese of UF#G and CE#G comes from the incomplete pickling process. After chemical exfoliation and high temperature reduction process, the intensity and width of the XRD peaks of rGO became weaker and broader, respectively, indicating the changes in the crystallization degree, defect degree and lamellar spacing of graphene (Fig. 2f) [43,50–55]. For graphite, the significant diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.431° is corresponded to (002) lattice plane with a d-spacing of 0.34 nm [50,52]. In the case of rGO (UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G), the position of the diffraction peak corresponding to (002) lattice plane moved to around 22°, indicating that the lamellar spacing of graphene increased to around 0.41 nm [51,52]. And the XRD peaks broadness of rGO indicates the existence of a range of crystallites, which can be ascribed to the incomplete decomposition of the GO nanosheets [51,53]. TGA and DTG results of the rGO nanosheets are depicted in Fig. 2g, heating to 800 ℃ with a ramp rate of 10 ℃•min−1 in a nitrogen environment. The inconspicuous mass degradation of the samples in the temperature between 200 and 600 ℃ is attributed to the decomposition of a small amount of oxygenated functional groups [56]. The dramatic weight loss of rGO and graphite between 600 and 800 ℃ is ascribed to the combustion or oxidative decomposition of the carbon skeleton [4,14,49,56]. Compared with graphite, the thermal stability of these rGO nanosheets (UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G) have been significantly degraded, which can be ascribed to the damage to the structure of rGO in the process of chemical exfoliation and thermal reduction [31,33,49]. The ultimate weight loss of UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G are 23.5%, 56.2%, and 42.6%, respectively, indicating that both curly edges and internal wrinkles defects will degrade the thermal stability of graphene.

The tribological properties of UF#G, CE#G and IW#G was investigated on a SRV4 friction and wear tester with ball-disc contact mode (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 3, 0.5 wt% UF#G can reduce the COF of P.G from 0.162 to 0.083 (decreased by 49%) under 25 ℃ and 150 N (Fig. 3 a). While the COFs of graphite, CE#G, and IW#G are 0.145, 0.132, and 0.123, respectively. The wear depth of the P.G-lubricated disc is approximately 2.78 μm, while that of UF#G is as low as 0.2 μm (Fig. S5 and S6, decreased by 93%). The optimal concentration of UF#G, CE#G, IW#G, and graphite are 0.5 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.3 wt% and 0.1 wt%, respectively (Fig. S7). When the concentration of graphene nanosheets is low, the supply of nanosheets at the interface finite domain is insufficient to achieve lubrication optimization. However, when the concentration of graphene nanosheets is too high, the nanosheets tend to agglomerate, and the interface lubrication performance is deteriorated [4,7,8,16]. The curly edge and internal wrinkle cause the increase of surface energy of graphene nanosheets, leading to more agglomeration of CE#G and IW#G nanosheets in the tribological zone [11,12,14,52]. Therefore, the optimal concentrations of CE#G and IW#G are lower than that of UF#G. Thus, the UF#G has much better lubricating performance than both CE#G and IW#G. Furthermore, the performances of rGOs under harsh conditions (high temperature, heavy load, and long cycle) are studied systematically in this study, which is a great challenge for functional materials.

UF#G has the lowest value of COF at different temperature (25–130 ℃) as shown in Fig. 3b. The COF values of the rGO nanosheets show fluctuation when temperature is above 100 ℃ according to the sudden increase in error bars of averaged COF. At high temperature, the stability of oil film decreases, and results in more interfacial asperities are in contact directly, thus leading to lubrication failure [57–60]. The bumped up of error bar is derived from severe friction of counterpart asperities, so the corresponding temperature is defined as the critical working temperature (CWT). The CWT of P.G is 100 ℃, while that of UF#G is 130 ℃, an increase of 30 ℃ relative to P.G. In addition, the CWT of graphite, CE#G, and IW#G are 100 ℃, 110 ℃, and 120 ℃, respectively. The wear scars of P.G and UF#G at different temperatures are shown in Fig. S8. The addition of UF#G can significantly reduce the width of wear scars, improving the anti-wear properties of interfaces.

Under different loads (50–250 N), these rGO nanosheets can effectively improve the lubrication performance of P.G (Fig. 3c). When the contact load increases to 250 N (2.98 GPa, much higher than the contact pressure of previous studies [4,6,7,61]), UF#G still has the excellent lubrication performance. In addition, the anti-friction (COF) and anti-wear (wear depth) properties of IW#G are slightly superior to that of CE#G (Fig. 3c and Table S5), indicating that the internal wrinkles have less influence on the lubrication performance of rGO nanosheets than the curly edges. Therefore, UF#G has wide load capacity and robust lubrication stability under heavy load. Further, the extreme pressure properties of the rGO nanosheets at ambient environment were also investigated (Fig. 3d). The addition of UF#G, IW#G, and CE#G can significantly enhance extreme pressure performance of grease, which can be attributed to the ultra-high normal bearing capacity and extremely low interlaminar slip energy barrier of graphene nanosheets [12,16,21]. With the addition of UF#G, the ultimate bearing capacity of the lubricant can be increased to at least 800 N (4.42 GPa). The addition of UF#G can reduce the scar depth from 12.5 μm (Fig. S9d5) to 2.6 μm (79.2%, Fig. S9d1), further meaning the significant enhancement for the extreme pressure performance of grease.

The long-operating behaviors (216,000 cycles, three hours) at high temperature (100 ℃) and heavy load (150 N, 2.5 Gpa) of the rGO nanosheets are shown in Fig. 3e. The COF of P.G and graphite increased and fluctuated dramatically during the whole friction cycles. The lubrication performances of IW#G and CE#G are better than P.G, but the COF values also severe fluctuate and gradually increase. UF#G (magenta line in Fig. 3e) has robust lubrication performance for long-cycle sliding (216,000 cycles, at least) under the high temperature (100 ℃), and the COF is robust and keeps a lowest level during the whole lubrication process. The wear scars after long-cycle friction are shown in Fig. S10. The addition of UF#G can improve the lubrication performance of grease greatly, reducing the diameter of wear spots of P.G from 1732 μm (Fig. S10a5) to 528 μm (69.5%, Fig. S10a1). It must be noted that UF#G has the longest life of anti-friction and anti-wear in previous studies for both nano-additives and nano-coatings [6,62–77], as shown in Table 1 . Compared with previous lubricating additives and coatings, UF#G has super-robust lubrication performances, especially at high temperature, heavy loads, and long cycles.

The lubricating medium after friction was collected, which dissolved in petroleum ether and processed by high-speed centrifugation. The residual debris can be obtained by extracting the sediment. The morphology and element distribution of wear debris of UF#G and P.G are shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S11, respectively (100 ℃, 150 N for 216,000 cycles). The debris of P.G is block with an average geometric size of 20–30 μm (Fig. 4a). While the abrasive debris of UF#G is granular with an average size of 0.2–0.4 μm (Fig. 4b). The significant differences in the morphology and size of the abrasive debris indicate that UF#G significantly enhances the anti-wear properties of the interfaces. Apart from some oxide of iron (Fig. 4c, Fig. S11c and 11d) in the debris, there are considerable solid particles containing calcium and sulfides (Fig. 4d). Calcium element is derived from saponifier in the P.G, and sulfur element comes from ZDDPs [6,7,58,60,61]. The formation of calcium sulfate indicates that ZDDPs has desulfurization reaction under mechanical stress and thermal activation [58,60]. The carbon elements enriched in the edge of the wear debris mainly come from the graphene debris after friction (Fig. 4e). The presence of graphene debris and calcium sulfate indicates that the interface lubrication properties are determined by the coupling of UF#G and base lubricant. The platinum element is derived from the conductive Pt film sprayed artificially, and the silicon element comes from the sample carrier of silicon wafer (Fig. 4e).

To further investigate the tribochemical reactions occurring in the friction process and explore the chemical valence of the tribological products, the XPS spectra of three kinds of rGO nanosheets and corresponding wear scars were conducted (Fig. S12). For carbon element, the content of chemical bonds before and after friction is shown in Table S6. After friction, the content of carbon oxygen bond (C-O and C = O bond) in the wear scars of UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G are 39.79%, 48.50%, and 49.31% (Fig. S12a2, b2, c2), respectively. For oxygen element, the content of chemical bonds before and after friction is shown in Table S7. Before friction, the oxygen element in graphene mainly exists in the form of O-C bond (Fig. S12a3, b3, c3). After friction, the content of O-C bond of wear scars decreases significantly, while a large amount of O-Fe bonds appears. And the content of O-Fe bond in wear scars of UF#G, CE#G, and IW#G are 29.89%, 42.17%, and 41.94%, respectively (Fig. S12a4, b4, c4). The above differences in the valence content of carbon, oxygen, and iron indicate that UF#G nanosheets can effectively inhabit the oxidation corrosion of the tribological interfaces. For iron element, the chemical composition of the wear scar after friction is shown in Table S8. The iron element in the wear scars mainly exists in the form of iron oxides (Fe2O3 and FeO), iron sulfides (Fe2(SO4)3 and FeSO4), iron carbide and elemental iron (Fig. S12a5, b5, c5). The presence of iron sulfides (Fe2(SO4)3 and FeSO4, Fig. S12 a4, b4, c4) and calcium sulfides (Fig. 4d) may indicate the formation of a sulfide layer on the substrate surface.

To verify the above hypothesis, the tribofilm morphology and chemical component of UF#G and P.G are characterized by HRTEM and EDS spectra (Fig. 5 ). The cross-section samples of wear scars for HRTEM are prepared by FIB (Fig. S13). The tribofilm thickness of UF#G is 120 nm approximately, while that of P.G is only about 60 nm (Fig. 5a and 5d). There is a remarkable stratification phenomenon in the wear scar region (Fig. 5b and 5e). From left to right are steel substrate (Ⅰ), CaSO4 layer (Ⅱ), amorphous carbon layer (III), sprayed chrome compound layer (IV), and Pt protective layer (Ⅴ), respectively. It seems that the tribofilm is mainly composed of CaSO4 layer (Fig. 5b Ⅱ and 5e Ⅱ) and amorphous carbon layer (Fig. 5b III and 5e III). The (020), (040), and (021) planes of CaSO4 with d-space of 0.748 nm, 0.378 nm, and 0.427 nm can be observed in the tribofilm after friction (Fig. 5c and 5f). The addition of UF#G can significantly enhance the robustness of amorphous carbon layer (the thickness of amorphous carbon layer (III) of UF#G (Fig. 5b) is 85 nm approximately, while that of P,G (Fig. 5e) is only 25 nm approximately). It may be ascribed to the fact that graphene transforms into amorphous carbon under induction of mechanical and thermal stress, which increases the robustness of amorphous carbon layer [3,6,12,60,61]. The HRTEM results confirm the presence of a layer of calcium sulfide on the friction interface. However, although the calcium sulfide layer can improve the lubrication performance of the interface, the lubrication performance of P.G is significantly inferior to that of UF#G (Fig. 3). Therefore, the effect of UF#G robust amorphous carbon layer on the interface lubrication properties remains to be further analyzed.

To investigate the structure evolution of rGO nanosheets induced by friction, Raman spectra was employed to detect the residual rGO nanosheets absorbed on the wear scars (Fig. S14). Ten detection points were selected at equal distance on the wear scars perpendicular to the direction of friction (Fig. S14a). The first and tenth points (No.1 and No.10) are located exterior of the wear scar, while the second to ninth points (No.2 to No.9) are located interior of the wear scar. The detection results of each point on the wear scar of UF#G are shown in Fig. S14b. Due to the difference in the content of rGO nanosheets absorbed at different positions on the wear scars, the signal strength detected at different points is significantly undulating. The intensities of D peaks and G peaks of three rGO nanosheets at different detected points are shown in Fig. S14c and Table S9. The ratio of D peak intensity and G peak intensity (ID/IG) is applied to characterized the defect degree of rGO nanosheets [29,78]. The results of ID/IG of three rGO nanosheets at each detecting point are shown in Fig. S14d and Table S9. From the No.1 to No.10 point, the value of ID/IG seems to decrease first and then increase. The minimum values of ID/IG of all three rGO nanosheets are obtained at the No.5 or No.6 detection points. This universal law indicates that the closer to the center of wear scars, the more regular the rGO nanosheets structure is, which may be attributed to the fact that the closer to the center of the wear scars, the rGO nanosheets bear less transverse shear stress (perpendicular to the frictional direction), thus less intralayer tearing occurs in the rGO nanosheets [16,79]. And the non-uniform broadening and passivation of the D and G peaks indicate that UF#G is developing towards amorphous carbon state containing 2D graphite layers and 3D graphite microcrystals [7,80]. The developing towards amorphous carbon of UF#G is mainly attributed to the fracture and irregular recombination of C–C bonds in UF#G under mechanical stress and thermal activation [49,80]. This explains why the amorphous carbon layer of UF#G tribofilm is significantly more robust than that of P.G (Fig. 5).

In order to investigate the physical morphology evolution of the three kinds of rGO nanosheets during friction, the rGO nanosheets after friction was extracted from the lubricating medium. The supernatant of lubricating medium after centrifugation was extracted and dropped on the silicon wafer to prepare SEM sample of the three rGO nanosheets after friction. As shown in Fig. S15 the worn UF#G nanosheets (Fig. S15a) keeps an ultra-flat lamellar structure as similar as the virgin one (Fig. 2a). Thus, the UF#G nanosheets can easily overcome the weak interlayer van der Waal forces and slip between layers during friction (Fig. 6 a). After working on the harsh conditions, there are only a few defects such as edge loss and tearing damage on the nanosheets (Fig. S15a). Because the worn CE#G nanosheets have curly edges, the nanosheets curl inwards under the induction of mechanical force to form graphene nano-rolls (Fig. S15b). For comparison, there are obvious internal wrinkles and nano-clusters on worn IW#G (Fig. S15c) and the worn-out graphite has some defects on the boundary and surface (Fig. S15d). Accordingly, the obvious formation of “roll-like” nanostructure of graphene for CE#G results in poor tribological properties (Fig. 6b). Virgin internal wrinkles of IW#G and friction-induced nano-clusters lead to the agglomeration of rGO nanosheets, thus exhibiting much worse tribological properties (Fig. 6c).

In summary, high-quality ultra-flat rGO (UF#G) was synthesized by a novel chemical exfoliation method with super-robust lubrication performances, especially under harsh conditions (high temperature, heavy load, and long cycles). UF#G can reduce the COF and wear depth of the lubricated interfaces by 49% and 93%, respectively (2.5 Gpa, compared with P.G). And the UF#G has robust lubricating stability with long-operating cycles of 216,000 cycles under 100 ℃. The robust performances are attributed to chemical-mixing tribofilm consisting of a CaSO4-deposited layer and a carbon-adsorbed layer. In addition, the UF#G nanosheets can easily overcome the weak interlayer van der Waal forces and slip between layers because of the ultra-flat lamellar structure. In contrast the presence of curly edges (CE#G) and internal wrinkles (IW#G) degenerate the lubrication performance of graphene. This work provides a novel and feasible method to fabricate high-quality ultra-flat graphene nanosheets for practical lubrication applications especially under harsh conditions.
